([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Großherzogtum Baden*) was a German [polity](/wiki/Polity "Polity") on the east bank of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). It originally existed as a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") from 1806 to 1871 and later as part of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") until 1918.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[2]](#cite_note-edmundcclingan-2)

The **Grand Duchy of Baden** ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Großherzogtum Baden*) was a German [polity](/wiki/Polity "Polity") on the east bank of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). It originally existed as a [sovereign state](/wiki/Sovereign_state "Sovereign state") from 1806 to 1871 and later as part of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") until 1918.[[1]](#cite_note-Britannica-1)[[2]](#cite_note-edmundcclingan-2)

The duchy's 12th-century origins were as a [margraviate](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Baden "Margraviate of Baden") that eventually split into two, [Baden-Durlach](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Baden-Durlach "Margraviate of Baden-Durlach") and [Baden-Baden](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Baden-Baden "Margraviate of Baden-Baden"), before being reunified in 1771. The territory grew and assumed its ducal status after the dissolution of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") but suffered a [revolution](/wiki/Baden_Revolution "Baden Revolution") in 1848, whose demands had been formulated in [Offenburg](/wiki/Offenburg "Offenburg") the previous year at a meeting now considered the first-ever democratic program in Germany.[[3]](#cite_note-3) With the collapse of the German Empire it became part of the [Weimar Republic](/wiki/Weimar_Republic "Weimar Republic") under the name [Republic of Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden").

The Grand Duchy of Baden was bordered to the north by the [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") and the [Grand Duchy of Hesse](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Hesse "Grand Duchy of Hesse"), to the west by the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"), to the south by [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"), and to the east mainly by the [Kingdom of Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg"). Its unofficial anthem has been the [Badnerlied](/wiki/Badnerlied "Badnerlied"), or Song of the People of Baden, which has four or five traditional verses and many more added: there are collections with up to 591 verses.

## Creation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Creation")]

Baden came into existence in the 12th century as the [Margraviate of Baden](/wiki/Margraviate_of_Baden "Margraviate of Baden") and subsequently split into various smaller territories that were unified in 1771. In 1803 Baden was raised to [Electoral dignity](/wiki/Electorate_of_Baden "Electorate of Baden") within the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"), with ecclesiastical and secular territories added to it during the [German mediatisation](/wiki/German_mediatisation "German mediatisation"). Upon the dissolution of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") in 1806, Baden became the much-enlarged Grand Duchy of Baden. In 1815 it joined the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation").

## French Revolution and Napoleon

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: French Revolution and Napoleon")]

The outbreak of the [French Revolutionary Wars](/wiki/French_Revolutionary_Wars "French Revolutionary Wars") in 1792 saw Baden joining the [First Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_First_Coalition "War of the First Coalition") against [France](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). The conflict devastated the margraviate's countryside. Baden was defeated in 1796 with Margrave [Charles Frederick](/wiki/Charles_Frederick,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Charles Frederick, Grand Duke of Baden") being compelled to pay an [indemnity](/wiki/Indemnity "Indemnity") and cede his territories on the left bank of the Rhine to France. In 1803, largely owing to the good offices of [Tsar Alexander I](/wiki/Alexander_I_of_Russia "Alexander I of Russia") of [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), Charles Frederick received the [Prince-Bishopric of Constance](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Constance "Prince-Bishopric of Constance"), part of the [Rhenish Palatinate](/wiki/Electoral_Palatinate "Electoral Palatinate"), and other smaller districts, together with the dignity of a [prince-elector](/wiki/Prince-elector "Prince-elector"). Baden then changed sides in 1805 to join France under [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon") in the [War of the Third Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Third_Coalition "War of the Third Coalition"). France and its allies won the war, and in the [Peace of Pressburg](/wiki/Peace_of_Pressburg_(1805) "Peace of Pressburg (1805)") the same year, Baden obtained the [Breisgau](/wiki/Breisgau "Breisgau") and other territories in [Further Austria](/wiki/Further_Austria "Further Austria") at the expense of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). In 1806, Charles Frederick joined the [Confederation of the Rhine](/wiki/Confederation_of_the_Rhine "Confederation of the Rhine"), declared himself a sovereign prince and [grand duke](/wiki/Grand_duke "Grand duke"), and received additional territory.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Baden continued to assist France militarily, and by the [Treaty of Schönbrunn](/wiki/Treaty_of_Sch%C3%B6nbrunn "Treaty of Schönbrunn") in 1809, it was rewarded with accessions of territory at the expense of the [Kingdom of Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg"). Having quadrupled the area of Baden, Charles Frederick died in June 1811, and was succeeded by his grandson [Charles](/wiki/Charles,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Charles, Grand Duke of Baden"), who was married to [Stéphanie de Beauharnais](/wiki/St%C3%A9phanie_de_Beauharnais "Stéphanie de Beauharnais") (1789–1860) a cousin of French empress [Josephine's first husband](/wiki/Alexandre_de_Beauharnais "Alexandre de Beauharnais") and adopted daughter of Napoleon.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The [Napoleonic Code](/wiki/Napoleonic_Code "Napoleonic Code") was adopted in 1810, and remained in force until the adoption of the *[Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch](/wiki/B%C3%BCrgerliches_Gesetzbuch "Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch")* in 1900.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall201218-5)

Charles fought for his father-in-law until after the [Battle of Leipzig](/wiki/Battle_of_Leipzig "Battle of Leipzig") in 1813, when he joined the [Sixth Coalition](/wiki/War_of_the_Sixth_Coalition "War of the Sixth Coalition").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Baden in the German Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Baden in the German Confederation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/Karlsruhe_Constitution_Obelisk.JPG/250px-Karlsruhe_Constitution_Obelisk.JPG)](/wiki/File:Karlsruhe_Constitution_Obelisk.JPG)

Monument to the Constitution of Baden (and the Grand Duke for granting it), in Rondellplatz, Karlsruhe, Germany

In 1815 Baden became a member of the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") established by the Act of 8 June, annexed to the Final Act of the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") of 9 June. However, in the haste of winding up the Congress, the question of the succession to the grand duchy did not get settled, a matter that would soon become acute.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The [treaty of 16 April 1816](/wiki/Treaty_of_Munich_(1816) "Treaty of Munich (1816)"), by which the territorial disputes between [Austria](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire") and [Bavaria](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Bavaria "Kingdom of Bavaria") were settled, also supported Bavaria's claim to the Palatine parts of Baden on the east bank of the Rhine and reaffirmed the succession rights of King [Maximilian I Joseph of Bavaria](/wiki/Maximilian_I_Joseph_of_Bavaria "Maximilian I Joseph of Bavaria") and his [House of Palatinate-Birkenfeld](/wiki/House_of_Palatinate-Birkenfeld "House of Palatinate-Birkenfeld") to all of Baden, upon the expected event of the extinction of the [House of Zähringen](/wiki/House_of_Z%C3%A4hringen "House of Zähringen"). As a counter to this, in 1817, the Grand Duke Charles issued a [pragmatic sanction](/wiki/Pragmatic_sanction "Pragmatic sanction") (*Hausgesetz*) declaring the counts of Hochberg, the issue of a [morganatic marriage](/wiki/Morganatic_marriage "Morganatic marriage") between the Grand Duke Charles Frederick and [Luise Geyer von Geyersberg](/wiki/Luise_Geyer_von_Geyersberg "Luise Geyer von Geyersberg") (created countess Hochberg), capable of succeeding to the crown. A controversy between Bavaria and Baden ensued, which was only decided in favour of the Hochberg claims by a treaty signed by Baden and the four great powers at [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") on 10 July 1819.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Meanwhile, the dispute had wide-ranging effects. In order to secure popular support for the Hochberg heir, in 1818 Grand Duke Charles granted to the grand duchy, under Article XIII of the [Act of Confederation](/wiki/Constitution_of_the_German_Confederation "Constitution of the German Confederation"), a liberal [constitution](/wiki/Constitution "Constitution"), under which two chambers were constituted and their assent declared necessary for [legislation](/wiki/Legislation "Legislation") and [taxation](/wiki/Taxation "Taxation"). The outcome was important far beyond the narrow limits of the duchy, as all of Germany watched the constitutional experiments in the southern states.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In Baden, the conditions were not favourable for success. During the revolutionary period, the people had fallen completely under the influence of French ideas, and this was sufficiently illustrated by the temper of the new chambers, which tended to model their activity on the proceedings of the [National Convention](/wiki/National_Convention "National Convention") (1792–1795) in the earlier days of the French Revolution. Additionally, the new Grand Duke [Louis I](/wiki/Louis_I,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Louis I, Grand Duke of Baden") (ruled 1818–1830), who had succeeded in 1818, was unpopular, and the administration was in the hands of hide-bound and inefficient [bureaucrats](/wiki/Bureaucrat "Bureaucrat").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The result was a deadlock. Even before the promulgation of the [Carlsbad Decrees](/wiki/Carlsbad_Decrees "Carlsbad Decrees") in October 1819, the grand duke had [prorogued](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/prorogue "wikt:prorogue") the chambers after three months of unproductive debate. The reaction that followed was as severe in Baden as elsewhere in Germany, and culminated in 1823 when, on the refusal of the chambers to vote on the military budget, the grand duke dissolved them and levied the taxes on his own authority. In January 1825, owing to official pressure, only three Liberals were returned to the chamber. A law was passed making the budget presentable only every three years, and the constitution ceased to have any active existence.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In 1830 Grand Duke Louis was succeeded by his half-brother Grand Duke [Leopold](/wiki/Leopold,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Leopold, Grand Duke of Baden") (ruled 1830–1852), the first of the Höchberg line. The [July Revolution](/wiki/July_Revolution "July Revolution") (1830) in France did not cause any disturbances in Baden, but the new grand duke showed liberal tendencies from the beginning. The elections of 1830 proceeded without interference, and resulted in the return of a Liberal majority. The next few years saw the introduction, under successive ministries, of liberal reforms in the constitution, in criminal and civil law, and in education. In 1832, the adhesion of Baden to the [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") *[Zollverein](/wiki/Zollverein "Zollverein")* did much for the material prosperity of the country.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## 1849 Baden Revolution

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1849 Baden Revolution")]

Main article: [Baden Revolution](/wiki/Baden_Revolution "Baden Revolution")

By 1847, [radicalism](/wiki/Radicalism_(historical) "Radicalism (historical)") once more began to raise its head in Baden. On 12 September 1847, a popular demonstration held at [Offenburg](/wiki/Offenburg "Offenburg") passed resolutions demanding the conversion of the regular army into a national [militia](/wiki/Militia "Militia"), which should take an oath to the constitution, as well as a [progressive income tax](/wiki/Progressive_income_tax "Progressive income tax"), and a fair adjustment of the interests of [capital](/wiki/Capital_(economics) "Capital (economics)") and [labour](/wiki/Labour_(economics) "Labour (economics)").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The news of the [revolution of February 1848 in Paris](/wiki/French_Revolution_of_1848 "French Revolution of 1848") brought agitation to a head. Numerous public meetings were held and the Offenburg programme was adopted. On 4 March 1848, under the influence of popular excitement, the lower chamber accepted this programme almost unanimously. As in other German states, the government bowed to the storm, proclaimed an [amnesty](/wiki/Amnesty "Amnesty") and promised reforms. The ministry remodelled itself in a more liberal direction, and sent a new delegate to the federal diet at [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt"), empowered to vote for the establishment of a [parliament](/wiki/Parliament "Parliament") for a [united Germany](/wiki/Frankfurt_Parliament "Frankfurt Parliament").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Disorder, fomented by republican agitators, continued nonetheless. The efforts of the government to suppress the agitators with the aid of federal troops led to an armed insurrection, which was mastered without much difficulty. The [uprising](/wiki/Hecker_Uprising "Hecker Uprising"), led by [Friedrich Hecker](/wiki/Friedrich_Hecker "Friedrich Hecker"), [Gustav Struve](/wiki/Gustav_Struve "Gustav Struve") and others, was lost at [Kandern](/wiki/Kandern "Kandern") on 20 April 1848. [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg "Freiburg"), which they held, fell on 24 April and, on 27 April, a Franco–German *legion*, which had invaded Baden from [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg"), was routed at Dossenbach.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In the beginning of 1849, however, the issue of a new constitution in accordance with the resolutions of the Frankfurt parliament, led to more serious trouble. It did little to satisfy the radicals, angered by the refusal of the second chamber to agree to their proposal for the summoning of a [constituent assembly](/wiki/Constituent_Assembly "Constituent Assembly") on 10 February, 1849.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The new [insurrection](/wiki/Insurrection "Insurrection") that broke out proved a more formidable affair than the first. A military [mutiny](/wiki/Mutiny "Mutiny") at [Rastatt](/wiki/Rastatt "Rastatt") on 11 May showed that the army sympathised with the revolution, which was proclaimed two days later at Offenburg amid tumultuous scenes. Also, on 13 May a mutiny at [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe") forced Grand Duke Leopold to flee, and the next day his ministers followed. Meanwhile, a committee of the [diet](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag") under [Lorenz Brentano](/wiki/Lorenz_Brentano "Lorenz Brentano") (1813–1891), who represented the more moderate radicals against the republicans, established itself in the capital in an attempt to direct affairs pending the establishment of a provisional government.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

This was accomplished on 1 June and, on 10 June, the *constituent diet*, consisting entirely of the most "advanced" politicians, assembled. It had little chance of doing more than make speeches. The country remained in the hands of an armed mob of civilians and mutinous soldiers. Meanwhile, the Grand Duke of Baden had joined with Bavaria in requesting the armed intervention of Prussia, which [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") granted on the condition that Baden would join the [Alliance of the Three Kings](/wiki/Erfurt_Union "Erfurt Union").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

From this moment, the revolution in Baden was doomed, and with it the revolution across Germany. The [Prussian Army](/wiki/Prussian_Army "Prussian Army"), under Prince William (afterwards [William I, German Emperor](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor")), invaded Baden in the middle of June 1849.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4) Afraid of a military escalation, Brentano reacted hesitantly – too hesitantly for the more radical [Gustav Struve](/wiki/Gustav_Struve "Gustav Struve") and his followers, who overthrew him and established a Pole, [Ludwig Mieroslawski](/wiki/Ludwig_Mieroslawski "Ludwig Mieroslawski") (1814–1878), in his place.

Mieroslawski reduced the insurgents to some semblance of order. On 20 June, 1849, he met the Prussians at [Waghausel](/wiki/Waghausel "Waghausel"), and suffered complete defeat. On 25 June, Prince William entered Karlsruhe and, at the end of the month, the members of the provisional government, who had taken refuge at [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg_im_Breisgau "Freiburg im Breisgau"), dispersed. The insurgent leaders who were caught, notably the ex-officers, suffered military execution. The army was dispersed among Prussian garrison towns, and Prussian troops occupied Baden for a time.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4) In the months following, no fewer than 80,000 people left Baden for America.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Many of these migrants would later participate in the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") as abolitionists and union soldiers.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

Grand Duke Leopold returned on 10 August and at once dissolved the diet. The following elections resulted in a majority favourable to the new ministry, which passed a series of laws of a reactionary tendency with a view to strengthening the government.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Towards the German Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Towards the German Empire")]

Grand Duke Leopold died on 24 April 1852 and was succeeded by his second son, [Frederick](/wiki/Frederick_I,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Frederick I, Grand Duke of Baden"), as regent, since the eldest, [Louis II, Grand Duke of Baden](/wiki/Louis_II,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Louis II, Grand Duke of Baden") (died 22 January 1858), was incapable of ruling. The internal affairs of Baden during the period that followed have little general interest.

Grand Duke [Frederick I](/wiki/Frederick_I,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Frederick I, Grand Duke of Baden") (ruled 1856–1907) opposed the war with Prussia from the first, but yielded to popular resentment at the policy of Prussia on the [Schleswig-Holstein](/wiki/Schleswig-Holstein "Schleswig-Holstein") question. The ministry, as one, resigned. In the [Austro-Prussian War](/wiki/Austro-Prussian_War "Austro-Prussian War") of 1866, Austria's contingents, under Prince William, had two sharp engagements with the Prussian [Army of the Main](/wiki/Campaign_of_the_Main "Campaign of the Main"). However, on 24 July 1866, two days before the [Battle of Werbach](/wiki/Battle_of_Werbach "Battle of Werbach"), the second chamber petitioned the grand duke to end the war and enter into an offensive and defensive alliance with Prussia.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Baden announced her withdrawal from the [German Confederation](/wiki/German_Confederation "German Confederation") and, on 17 August 1866, signed a treaty of peace and alliance with Prussia. [Bismarck](/wiki/Otto_von_Bismarck "Otto von Bismarck") himself resisted the adhesion of Baden to the [North German Confederation](/wiki/North_German_Confederation "North German Confederation"). He had no wish to give [Napoleon III](/wiki/Napoleon_III "Napoleon III") a good excuse for intervention, but the opposition of Baden to the formation of a South German confederation made the union inevitable. The [Baden army](/w/index.php?title=Baden_army&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baden army (page does not exist)") took a conspicuous share in the [Franco-Prussian War](/wiki/Franco-Prussian_War "Franco-Prussian War") of 1870, and it was Grand Duke Frederick of Baden, who, in the historic assembly of the German princes at [Versailles](/wiki/Palace_of_Versailles "Palace of Versailles"), was the first to hail the king of Prussia as [German Emperor](/wiki/German_Emperor "German Emperor").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## *Kulturkampf*

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Kulturkampf")]

The internal politics of Baden, both before and after 1870, centered in the main around the question of [religion](/wiki/Religion "Religion"). The signing on 28 June 1859 of a [concordat](/wiki/Concordat "Concordat") with the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), which placed [education](/wiki/Education "Education") under the oversight of the [clergy](/wiki/Clergy "Clergy") and facilitated the establishment of [religious institutes](/wiki/Religious_institute "Religious institute"), led to a constitutional struggle. This struggle ended in 1863 with the victory of [secular](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") principles, making the [communes](/wiki/Commune_(subnational_entity) "Commune (subnational entity)") responsible for education, though admitting the priests to a share in the management. The quarrel between secularism and [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), however, did not end. In 1867, on the accession to the premiership of [Julius von Jolly (1823–1891)](/wiki/Julius_Jolly_(politician) "Julius Jolly (politician)"), several constitutional changes in a secular direction occurred: [responsibility](/wiki/Social_responsibility "Social responsibility") of ministers, [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press"), and compulsory education. On 6 September 1867, a law compelled all candidates for the priesthood to pass government examinations. [The archbishop of Freiburg](/wiki/Hermann_von_Vicari "Hermann von Vicari") resisted, and, on his death in April 1868, [the see](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Freiburg "Archdiocese of Freiburg") remained vacant.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In 1869, the introduction of [civil marriage](/wiki/Civil_marriage "Civil marriage") did not allay the strife, which reached its climax after the proclamation of the [dogma](/wiki/Dogma "Dogma") of [papal infallibility](/wiki/Papal_infallibility "Papal infallibility") in 1870. The *[Kulturkampf](/wiki/Kulturkampf "Kulturkampf")* raged in Baden, as in the rest of Germany, and, here as elsewhere, the government encouraged the formation of [Old Catholic](/wiki/Old_Catholic "Old Catholic") communities. Not until 1880, after the fall of the ministry of Jolly, did Baden reconcile with Rome. In 1882 the archbishopric of Freiburg was again filled.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## In the German Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: In the German Empire")]

The political tendency of Baden, meanwhile, mirrored that of all Germany. In 1892 the [National Liberals](/wiki/National_Liberal_Party_(Germany) "National Liberal Party (Germany)") had a majority of just one in the diet. From 1893, they could stay in power only with the aid of the Conservatives and, in 1897, a coalition of the Catholic [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Germany) "Centre Party (Germany)"), [Socialists](/wiki/Socialists "Socialists"), [Social Democrats](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Social Democratic Party of Germany") and [Radicals](/wiki/Radicals_(UK) "Radicals (UK)") (*Freisinnige*) won a majority for the opposition in the chamber.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Amid all these contests, the statesmanlike moderation of Grand Duke Frederick won him universal esteem. By the treaty under which Baden had become an integral part of the [German Empire](/wiki/German_Empire "German Empire") in 1871, he had reserved only the exclusive right to tax beer and [spirits](/wiki/Distilled_beverage "Distilled beverage"). The army, the post office, railways and the conduct of foreign relations passed to the effective control of Prussia.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In his relations with the German Empire, too, Frederick proved himself more of a great German noble than a sovereign prince actuated by [particularist](/wiki/Political_particularism "Political particularism") ambitions. His position as husband of the Emperor [William I](/wiki/William_I,_German_Emperor "William I, German Emperor")'s only daughter, [Louise](/wiki/Princess_Louise_of_Prussia "Princess Louise of Prussia") (whom he had married in 1856), gave him a peculiar influence in the councils of Berlin. When, on 20 September 1906, the Grand Duke celebrated at once the jubilee of his reign and his golden wedding anniversary, all Europe honoured him. King [Edward VII](/wiki/Edward_VII "Edward VII") appointed him, by the hands of the [Duke of Connaught](/wiki/Prince_Arthur,_Duke_of_Connaught_and_Strathearn "Prince Arthur, Duke of Connaught and Strathearn"), a Knight of the [Order of the Garter](/wiki/Order_of_the_Garter "Order of the Garter"). But more significant, perhaps, was the tribute paid by *[Le Temps](/wiki/Le_Temps "Le Temps")*, the leading Parisian paper:[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

> Nothing more clearly demonstrates the sterile paradox of the Napoleonic work than the history of the Grand Duchy. It was [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), and he alone, who created this whole state in 1803 to reward in the person of the little margrave of Baden a relative of the [emperor of Russia](/wiki/Emperor_of_Russia "Emperor of Russia"). It was he who after [Austerlitz](/wiki/Battle_of_Austerlitz "Battle of Austerlitz") aggrandized the margravate at the expense of Austria; transformed it into a sovereign principality and raised it to a Grand Duchy. It was he too who, by the secularization on the one hand and by the dismemberment of [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg") on the other, gave the Grand Duke 500,000 new subjects. He believed that the recognition of the prince and the artificial ethnical formation of the principality would be pledges of security for France. But in 1813 Baden joined the coalition, and since then that nation created of odds and ends (*de bric et de broc*) and always handsomely treated by us, had not ceased to take a leading part in the struggles against our country. The Grand Duke Frederick, Grand Duke by the will of Napoleon, has done France all the harm he could. But French opinion itself renders justice to the probity of his character and to the ardour of his patriotism, and nobody will feel surprise at the homage with which Germany feels bound to surround his old age.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Grand Duke Frederick I died at [Mainau](/wiki/Mainau "Mainau") on 28 September 1907. He was succeeded by his son, Grand Duke [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Frederick II, Grand Duke of Baden")[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4) (ruled 1907–1918, died 1928). His wife, [Princess Hilda of Nassau](/wiki/Princess_Hilda_of_Nassau "Princess Hilda of Nassau"), was popular due her supports of artistic endeavors and treatment of the wounded soldiers during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I").[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall2012123-8)

22,000 industrial workers in [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") went on strike in January 1918.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall201288-9) Members of the garrisons of Lahr and Offenburg formed a soldiers' council on 8 November 1918. [Wilhelm Engelbert Oeftering](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wilhelm_Engelbert_Oeftering "de:Wilhelm Engelbert Oeftering") stated that a member of Replacement Battalion 171 who returned from [Kiel](/wiki/Kiel_mutiny "Kiel mutiny") initiated the demonstration. Another soldiers' council was formed in Mannheim on 9 November.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall2012100-102-10)

[![Abdication certificate of Frederick II](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Frederick_II_abdication_certificate.png/250px-Frederick_II_abdication_certificate.png)](/wiki/File:Frederick_II_abdication_certificate.png)

The abdication certificate of [Frederick II](/wiki/Frederick_II,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Frederick II, Grand Duke of Baden")

The NLP, Centre, and [Progressive](/wiki/Progressive_People%27s_Party_(Germany) "Progressive People's Party (Germany)") (FVP) parties requested President [Heinrich Bodman](/w/index.php?title=Heinrich_Bodman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Heinrich Bodman (page does not exist)")'s resignation on 9 November, due to fears that violence would break out in Mannheim. Kaiser [Wilhelm II](/wiki/Wilhelm_II "Wilhelm II") abdicated the same day while the mayors of [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") and [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe") formed Welfare Committees. The Karlsruhe Welfare Committee and [Soviets](/wiki/Soviet_(council) "Soviet (council)") formed a provisional government, with Bodman's recognition, on 10 November. Five SPD members, two [Independent Social Democrats](/wiki/Independent_Social_Democratic_Party_of_Germany "Independent Social Democratic Party of Germany"), two Centrists, and two National Liberals made up the government. [Anton Geiss](/wiki/Anton_Geiss "Anton Geiss"), the chair of the SPD in Baden and vice-president of the Landtag, chaired the provisional government.[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEExner2016291-294-11)[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrill198315-16-12)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

From 10 to 14 November, soldiers' councils called for the government to declare a republic and remove Frederick II. Frederick II called this government unconstitutional, but did not contest it and dismissed his ministers. He renounced his governmental powers on 13 November, and abdicated on 22 November, ending 600 years of rule by the [House of Zähringen](/wiki/House_of_Z%C3%A4hringen "House of Zähringen"). He was one of the last German monarchs to abdicate.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall2012123-127-14)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEExner2016291-294-11) The [Free People's Republic of Baden](/wiki/Republic_of_Baden "Republic of Baden") was formed on 14 November, and elections scheduled on 5 January 1919.[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGrill198316-15)

## Legacy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Legacy")]

After [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") the French military government created the [State of Baden](/wiki/South_Baden "South Baden"), at first called South Baden, out of the southern half of the former duchy, with [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg_im_Breisgau "Freiburg im Breisgau") as its capital; this area was declared in its 1947 constitution to be the true successor of the duchy. The northern half was combined with northern [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg"), becoming part of the [American-occupied zone](/wiki/American-occupied_zone_of_Germany "American-occupied zone of Germany") and forming the state of [Württemberg-Baden](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Baden "Württemberg-Baden"). Both Baden and Württemberg-Baden became states of [West Germany](/wiki/West_Germany "West Germany") upon the latter's formation in 1949, but in 1952 they merged with each other and with [Württemberg-Hohenzollern](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg-Hohenzollern "Württemberg-Hohenzollern"), which was southern Württemberg and a former [Prussian](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia") exclave, to form [Baden-Württemberg](/wiki/Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg "Baden-Württemberg") – still the only merger of states to have taken place in the history of the [Federal Republic of Germany](/wiki/Federal_Republic_of_Germany "Federal Republic of Germany").

## Constitution and government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Constitution and government")]

A constitution was adopted on 22 August 1814, and existed with some changes until the end of the duchy.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESchmidgall201219-16) The Grand Duchy of Baden was a hereditary [monarchy](/wiki/Monarchy "Monarchy") with executive power vested in the Grand Duke; legislative authority was shared between him and a [representative assembly](/wiki/Representative_assembly "Representative assembly") (*[Landtag](/wiki/Landtag "Landtag")*) consisting of two chambers.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The upper chamber included all the princes of the ruling family of full age, the heads of all the mediatized families, the [Archbishop of Freiburg](/wiki/Archbishop_of_Freiburg "Archbishop of Freiburg"), the president of the [Protestant Evangelical Church of Baden](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_in_Baden "Evangelical Church in Baden"), a deputy from each of the universities and the technical high school, eight members elected by the territorial nobility for four years, three representatives elected by the [chamber of commerce](/wiki/Chamber_of_commerce "Chamber of commerce"), two by that of agriculture, one by the trades, two mayors of municipalities, and eight members (two of them legal functionaries) nominated by the Grand Duke.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The lower chamber consisted of 73 popular representatives, of whom 24 were elected by the burgesses of certain communities, and 49 by rural communities. Every citizen of 25 years of age, who had not been convicted and was not a [pauper](/wiki/Pauper "Pauper"), had a vote. The elections were, however, indirect. The citizens selected the *Wahlmänner* (deputy [electors](/wiki/Indirect_election "Indirect election")), the latter selecting the representatives. The chambers met at least every two years. The lower chambers were elected for four years, half the members retiring every two years.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The executive consisted of four departments: the interior, foreign and grand-ducal affairs; finance; justice; and ecclesiastical affairs and education.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The chief sources of revenue were direct and indirect [taxes](/wiki/Tax "Tax"), the [railways](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden_State_Railways "Grand Duchy of Baden State Railways") and domains. The railways were operated by the state, and formed the only source of major public debt, about 22 million pounds sterling.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The supreme courts lay in [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe"), [Freiburg](/wiki/Freiburg "Freiburg"), [Offenburg](/wiki/Offenburg "Offenburg"), [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg"), [Mosbach](/wiki/Mosbach "Mosbach"), [Waldshut](/wiki/Waldshut-Tiengen "Waldshut-Tiengen"), [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz "Konstanz"), and [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim"), from which appeals passed to the *[Reichsgericht](/wiki/Reichsgericht "Reichsgericht")* (the supreme tribunal) in [Leipzig](/wiki/Leipzig "Leipzig").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Population")]

At the beginning of the 19th century, Baden was a [margraviate](/wiki/Margraviate "Margraviate"), with an area of barely 1,300 sq mi (3,400 km2) and a population of 210,000. Subsequently, the grand duchy acquired more territory so that, by 1905, it had 5,823 sq mi (15,082 km2)[[17]](#cite_note-Catholic-17) and a population of 2,010,728.[[17]](#cite_note-Catholic-17) Of that number, 61% were [Roman Catholics](/wiki/Roman_Catholics "Roman Catholics"), 37% [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant"), 1.5% [Jews](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and the remainder of other religions. At that time, about half of the population was rural, living in communities of less than 2,000; the density of the rest was about 330/sq mi (130/km2).[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The country was divided into the following districts:[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

* [Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") district had the towns Mannheim, and [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg "Heidelberg")
* [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe_(district) "Karlsruhe (district)") district included [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe "Karlsruhe") and [Pforzheim](/wiki/Pforzheim "Pforzheim")
* [Freiburg im Breisgau](/wiki/Freiburg_im_Breisgau "Freiburg im Breisgau") district included Freiburg
* [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz_(district) "Konstanz (district)") district had [Konstanz](/wiki/Konstanz "Konstanz")

The capital of the duchy was Karlsruhe, and important towns other than those listed included [Rastatt](/wiki/Rastatt "Rastatt"), [Baden-Baden](/wiki/Baden-Baden "Baden-Baden"), [Bruchsal](/wiki/Bruchsal "Bruchsal"), [Lahr](/wiki/Lahr "Lahr") and [Offenburg](/wiki/Offenburg "Offenburg"). The population was most thickly clustered in the north and near the Swiss city of [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"). The inhabitants of Baden are of various origins, those to the south of [Murg](/wiki/Murg_(Northern_Black_Forest) "Murg (Northern Black Forest)") being descended from the [Alemanni](/wiki/Alemanni "Alemanni") and those to the north from the [Franks](/wiki/Franks "Franks"), while the [Swabian Plateau](/w/index.php?title=Swabian_Plateau&action=edit&redlink=1 "Swabian Plateau (page does not exist)") derives its name from the adjacent German tribe ([Schwaben](/wiki/Schwaben "Schwaben")),[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4) who lived in [Württemberg](/wiki/W%C3%BCrttemberg "Württemberg").

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Map_of_Baden_%281819-1945%29.png/250px-Map_of_Baden_%281819-1945%29.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Baden_(1819-1945).png)

Baden from 1819 to 1945: 

---

Grand Duchy of Baden

[County Palatine of the Rhine](/wiki/County_Palatine_of_the_Rhine "County Palatine of the Rhine") (part of [Kingdom of Bavaria](/wiki/History_of_Bavaria#Kingdom_of_Bavaria "History of Bavaria"))

[Grand Duchy of Hesse](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Hesse "Grand Duchy of Hesse")

[Hohenzollern](/wiki/Province_of_Hohenzollern "Province of Hohenzollern") (part of [Kingdom of Prussia](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Prussia "Kingdom of Prussia") from 1850)

[Kingdom of Württemberg](/wiki/Kingdom_of_W%C3%BCrttemberg "Kingdom of Württemberg")

---

*[French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire") ([Kingdom](/wiki/Bourbon_Restoration_in_France "Bourbon Restoration in France") from 1814–48, etc)*

*[Swiss Confederation](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland")*

The grand duchy had an area of 15,081 km2 (5,823 sq mi)[[17]](#cite_note-Catholic-17) and consisted of a considerable portion of the eastern half of the fertile valley of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") and of the mountains which form its boundary.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The mountainous part was by far the most extensive, forming nearly 80% of the whole area. From [Lake Constance](/wiki/Lake_Constance "Lake Constance") in the south to the river [Neckar](/wiki/Neckar "Neckar") in the north is a portion of the [Black Forest](/wiki/Black_Forest "Black Forest") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Schwarzwald*), which is divided by the valley of the [Kinzig](/wiki/Kinzig_(Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg) "Kinzig (Baden-Württemberg)") into two districts of different elevation. To the south of the Kinzig the mean height is 945 m (3,100 ft)), and the highest summit, the [Feldberg](/wiki/Feldberg_(Black_Forest) "Feldberg (Black Forest)"), reaches about 1,493 m (4,898 ft), while to the north the mean height is only 640 metres (2,100 ft), and the [Hornisgrinde](/wiki/Hornisgrinde "Hornisgrinde"), the culminating point of the whole, does not exceed 1,164 metres (3,819 ft). To the north of the Neckar is the [Odenwald](/wiki/Odenwald "Odenwald") Range, with a mean of 439 metres (1,440 ft), and in the [Katzenbuckel](/wiki/Katzenbuckel "Katzenbuckel"), an extreme of 603 metres (1,978 ft). Lying between the Rhine and the [Dreisam](/wiki/Dreisam "Dreisam") is the [Kaiserstuhl](/wiki/Kaiserstuhl_(Baden-W%C3%BCrttemberg) "Kaiserstuhl (Baden-Württemberg)"), an independent [volcanic](/wiki/Volcanic "Volcanic") group, nearly 16 km in length and 8 km in breadth, the highest point of which is 536 metres (1,759 ft).[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The greater part of Baden belongs to the basin of the Rhine, which receives upwards of twenty tributaries from the highlands; the north-eastern portion of the territory is also watered by the [Main](/wiki/Main_(river) "Main (river)") and the Neckar. A part, however, of the eastern slope of the Black Forest belongs to the basin of the [Danube](/wiki/Danube "Danube"), which there takes its rise in a number of mountain streams. Among the numerous lakes which belonged to the duchy are the [Mummelsee](/wiki/Mummelsee "Mummelsee"), [Wildersee](/w/index.php?title=Wildersee&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wildersee (page does not exist)"), [Eichenersee](/w/index.php?title=Eichenersee&action=edit&redlink=1 "Eichenersee (page does not exist)") and [Schluchsee](/wiki/Schluchsee "Schluchsee"), but none of them is of any significant size. [Lake Constance](/wiki/Lake_Constance "Lake Constance") (*Bodensee*) belongs partly to the [German federal states](/wiki/States_of_Germany "States of Germany") (*Länder*) of Baden-Württemberg and Bavaria, and partly to [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

Owing to its physical configuration, Baden presents great extremes of heat and cold. The Rhine valley is the warmest district in Germany, but the higher elevations of the Black Forest record the greatest degrees of cold experienced in the south. The mean temperature of the Rhine valley is approximately 10 °C (50 °F) and that of the high [table-land](/wiki/Table_(landform) "Table (landform)") 6 °C (43 °F). July is the hottest month and January the coldest.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The [mineral](/wiki/Mineral "Mineral") wealth of Baden was not great, but [iron](/wiki/Iron "Iron"), [coal](/wiki/Coal "Coal"), [lead](/wiki/Lead "Lead") and [zinc](/wiki/Zinc "Zinc") of excellent quality were produced; [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver"), [copper](/wiki/Copper "Copper"), [gold](/wiki/Gold "Gold"), [cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt"), [vitriol](/wiki/Vitriol "Vitriol") and [sulfur](/wiki/Sulfur "Sulfur") were obtained in small quantities. [Peat](/wiki/Peat "Peat") was found in abundance, as well as [gypsum](/wiki/Gypsum "Gypsum"), china [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), [potter's earth](/w/index.php?title=Potter%27s_earth&action=edit&redlink=1 "Potter's earth (page does not exist)") and [salt](/wiki/Salt "Salt"). The [mineral springs](/wiki/Mineral_spring "Mineral spring") of Baden are still very numerous and have acquired great celebrity, those of [Baden-Baden](/wiki/Baden-Baden "Baden-Baden"), [Badenweiler](/wiki/Badenweiler "Badenweiler"), [Antogast](/w/index.php?title=Antogast&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antogast (page does not exist)"), [Griesbach](/wiki/Bad_Griesbach_(Rottal) "Bad Griesbach (Rottal)"), [Friersbach](/w/index.php?title=Friersbach&action=edit&redlink=1 "Friersbach (page does not exist)") and [Peterthal](/w/index.php?title=Peterthal&action=edit&redlink=1 "Peterthal (page does not exist)") being the most frequented.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

In the valleys the soil is particularly fertile, yielding luxuriant crops of wheat, maize, barley, [spelt](/wiki/Spelt "Spelt"), [rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye"), [beans](/wiki/Bean "Bean"), [potatoes](/wiki/Potato "Potato"), [flax](/wiki/Flax "Flax"), [hemp](/wiki/Hemp "Hemp"), [hops](/wiki/Hops "Hops"), [beetroot](/wiki/Beetroot "Beetroot") and tobacco; and even in the more mountainous part, [rye](/wiki/Rye "Rye"), wheat and [oats](/wiki/Oat "Oat") are extensively cultivated. There is a considerable extent of pasture-land, and the rearing of cattle, sheep, pigs and [goats](/wiki/Goat "Goat") is extensively practised. Of [game](/wiki/Game_(hunting) "Game (hunting)"), [deer](/wiki/Deer "Deer"), [boar](/wiki/Boar "Boar"), [snipe](/wiki/Snipe "Snipe") and wild [partridges](/wiki/Partridge "Partridge") are fairly abundant, while the mountain streams yield [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout") of excellent quality. Viticulture is increasing, and the [wines](/wiki/Wine "Wine") continue to sell well. The [Baden wine region](/wiki/Baden_(wine_region) "Baden (wine region)") is Germany's third largest in terms of vineyard surface. The [gardens](/wiki/Garden "Garden") and the [orchards](/wiki/Orchard "Orchard") supply an abundance of fruit, especially [sweet cherries](/wiki/Sweet_cherry "Sweet cherry"), [plums](/wiki/Plum "Plum"), [apples](/wiki/Apple "Apple") and [walnuts](/wiki/Walnut "Walnut"), and [bee-keeping](/wiki/Bee-keeping "Bee-keeping") is practised throughout the country. A greater proportion of Baden than any other south German state is occupied by forests. In these, the predominant trees are [European beech](/wiki/European_beech "European beech") and [silver fir](/wiki/Abies_alba "Abies alba"), but many others, such as [sweet chestnut](/wiki/Sweet_chestnut "Sweet chestnut"), [Scots pine](/wiki/Scots_pine "Scots pine"), [Norway spruce](/wiki/Norway_spruce "Norway spruce") and the exotic [coast Douglas-fir](/wiki/Coast_Douglas-fir "Coast Douglas-fir"), are well represented. A third, at least, of the annual [timber](/wiki/Timber "Timber") production is exported.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Army

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Army")]

Until 1871, the Grand Duchy maintained the [Baden Army](/wiki/Baden_Army "Baden Army") for defence.

## Industries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Industries")]

Around 1910, 56.8% of the region's land mass was cultivated and 38% was [forested](/wiki/Forested "Forested"). Before 1870, the [agricultural sector](/wiki/Agricultural_sector "Agricultural sector") was responsible for the bulk of the grand duchy's wealth, but this was superseded by industrial production. The chief products were [machinery](/wiki/Machinery "Machinery"), [woollen](/wiki/Wool "Wool") and [cotton](/wiki/Cotton "Cotton") goods, [silk](/wiki/Silk "Silk") ribbons, [paper](/wiki/Paper "Paper"), [tobacco](/wiki/Tobacco "Tobacco"), [china](/wiki/China_(material) "China (material)"), [leather](/wiki/Leather "Leather"), [glass](/wiki/Glass "Glass"), [clocks](/wiki/Clock "Clock"), [jewellery](/wiki/Jewellery "Jewellery"), and [chemicals](/wiki/Chemical "Chemical"). [Beet sugar](/wiki/Beet_sugar "Beet sugar") was also manufactured on a large scale, as were wooden [ornaments](/wiki/Wood_carving "Wood carving") and [toys](/wiki/Toy "Toy"), [music boxes](/wiki/Music_box "Music box") and [organs](/wiki/Organ_(music) "Organ (music)").[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

The [exports](/wiki/Export "Export") of Baden consisted mostly of the above goods and were considerable, but the bulk of its trade consisted of transit. The grand duchy had many [railways](/wiki/Railway "Railway") and roads,[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4) as well as the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine") for transporting goods by ship. Railways were run by the state as the [Grand Duchy of Baden State Railway](/wiki/Grand_Duchy_of_Baden_State_Railway "Grand Duchy of Baden State Railway") (*Großherzoglich Badische Staatseisenbahnen*). A rail-line ran mostly parallel with the Rhine, with oblique branches from East to West.

[Mannheim](/wiki/Mannheim "Mannheim") was the great market centre for exports down the Rhine and had substantial river traffic. It was also the chief manufacturing town for the duchy and an important administrative centre for its northern region.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Education and religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Education and religion")]

There were numerous educational institutions in Baden. There were three universities, one [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") in [Heidelberg](/wiki/Heidelberg_University "Heidelberg University"), one [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic") in [Freiburg im Breisgau](/wiki/University_of_Freiburg "University of Freiburg"), and a research university in [Karlsruhe](/wiki/Karlsruhe_Institute_of_Technology "Karlsruhe Institute of Technology").

The grand-duke was a Protestant; under him, the [Evangelical Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Church_in_Germany "Evangelical Church in Germany") was governed by a nominated council and a [synod](/wiki/Synod "Synod") consisting of a prelate, 48 elected and 7 nominated lay and clerical members. The Roman Catholic [Archbishop of Freiburg](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Freiburg "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Freiburg") is [Metropolitan](/wiki/Metropolitan_bishop "Metropolitan bishop") of the Upper Rhine.[[4]](#cite_note-EB1911-4)

## Leaders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Leaders")]

### Grand Dukes (1806–1918)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Grand_Duchy_of_Baden&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Grand Dukes (1806–1918)")]

See also: [List of monarchs of Baden](/wiki/List_of_monarchs_of_Baden "List of monarchs of Baden")

* 1806–1811: [Charles Frederick](/wiki/Charles_Frederick,_Grand_Duke_of_Baden "Charles Frederick, Grand Duke of Baden") (1728–1811)
